meta.title: Mercedes V250d V300d Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz V250d / V300d engine — OM654 2.0 diesel. W447 generation, real UK costs and reliability. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

@graph.name: Mercedes‑Benz V250d / V300d Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz V250d / V300d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM654 engine typically costs £3,000–£6,500 supply only for the V250d, and £4,000–£8,500 for the V300d. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600. A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz V250d / V300d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 is rated ★★★½☆. Long‑term tests show it can exceed 350,000 km (217,000+ miles) when properly maintained with high‑quality oil. However, it has known issues: camshaft and tappet wear, timing chain wear (subject to recall on some models), water pump failure, AdBlue system faults, and oil leaks. The engine is sensitive to oil quality and regular maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz V250d / V300d?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM654 camshaft and tappet wear (ticking noise), timing chain wear (cold‑start rattle — recall on some models), water pump failure (coolant loss, overheating), AdBlue system faults (NOx sensors, pump, level sensor), and oil leaks (valve cover gasket). The timing chain and water pump are the most critical — if either fails, the engine can be destroyed.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz V250d / V300d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well‑kept V‑Class worth £15,000–£30,000, a £4,000–£8,500 recon is a manageable 13–57% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a well‑maintained example. Given the V‑Class's strong retained values and popularity as a premium MPV, replacement is usually viable for vehicles in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz V250d / V300d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The V250d and V300d both use the OM654 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder diesel engine. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM654's timing chain, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A timing chain and tensioner replacement (£1,500–£4,000) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain failure (£3,500–£8,500 engine replacement). At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, book an inspection immediately. A recall was issued for certain OM654 engines where chain bolts or links could wear — check if your vehicle is affected.

eraMap.title: The V250d / V300d THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: First-generation OM654 in the V-Class — smooth and torquey, but camshaft wear, timing chain issues and AdBlue faults are the main watchpoints.
Facelift V250d — improved OM654 with 48V mild-hybrid; valvetrain wear and AdBlue system faults remain watchpoints.
The higher-output OM654 — 237 bhp, same engine architecture with higher boost and more emissions complexity.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz V250d / V300d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a V-Class diesel engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Engine Finders connects UK drivers with vetted specialists for reconditioned V-Class replacement engines. A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz V250d / V300d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: If caught early; don't drive with ticking noise
If caught early; recall may cover some models
Replace NOx sensors, pump or injector; don't let countdown expire
Replace gasket; common around valve cover
Check recall status — dealer will replace free of charge

buyingChecks.title: Buying This V250d / V300d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with documented oil changes (using MB 229.52 spec oil)
* Post‑2020 facelift — generally more refined than the pre‑facelift
* Evidence of water pump replacement — this is a key preventative item
* No AdBlue system issues or evidence of NOx sensor replacement
* Recall work completed (2022 fuel filter drainpipe recall)
avoidIf: * Ticking or tapping noise from the top of the engine — potential valvetrain wear
* Rattling on cold start — timing chain wear warning
* AdBlue countdown active — potential system failure
* Low coolant warnings with no diagnosis — water pump risk
* Poor service history — the OM654 is sensitive to oil quality

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The V250d / V300d's OM654 is Mercedes' most refined four-cylinder diesel — a smooth, torquey engine that delivers effortless performance in the premium MPV. Long‑term tests show it can exceed 350,000 km when properly maintained. However, it has known issues: camshaft and tappet wear, timing chain wear (subject to recall on some models), water pump failure, AdBlue system faults, and oil leaks. Buy on documented history and proof of preventative maintenance. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with a 12‑month warranty is the sweet spot.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz V250d / V300d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling noise on startup (timing chain wear warning — the most critical check)
* Ticking Noise — Any tapping or ticking from the top of the engine? Valvetrain wear indicator
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM654 is the only V250d/V300d engine — verify this before purchase) and oil change history (regular, high‑quality oil is essential)
* Water Pump — Any history of water pump replacement? This is a known weak point
* AdBlue System — Any AdBlue warnings? Countdown active? NOx sensor or pump failure
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil spots under the car — valve cover gasket leaks are common
* Recall Check — Has the 2022 fuel filter drainpipe recall been completed?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, AdBlue, or emissions issues?
